questions.question: What document establishes the missions, responsibilities, and geographic areas of responsibility for combatant commands?
options: The National Security Strategy
The Unified Command Plan (UCP)
The National Defense Authorization Act
The Joint Strategic Capabilities Plan

questions.question: Who has the authority to establish combatant commands and assign forces to those commands?
options: The President of the United States
The Secretary of Defense
The Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
The National Security Council

questions.question: Which of the following is a geographic combatant command?
options: U.S. Special Operations Command
U.S. Transportation Command
U.S. Strategic Command
U.S. Indo-Pacific Command

questions.question: Which of the following is a functional combatant command?
options: U.S. European Command
U.S. Central Command
U.S. Cyber Command
U.S. Africa Command

questions.question: What is the primary role of the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff (CJCS)?
options: To serve as the principal military advisor to the President, Secretary of Defense, and National Security Council
To approve the defense budget for all military services
To command all U.S. military forces in combat operations
To appoint combatant commanders and approve their operational plans

questions.question: The Joint Chiefs of Staff (JCS) consist of which members?
options: The Secretary of Defense, CJCS, and all four-star generals
The combatant commanders and their deputies
The CJCS, Vice CJCS, and the Service Chiefs of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Space Force
The CJCS and the commanders of all geographic combatant commands

questions.question: Does the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff exercise military command over any combatant command or armed force?
options: Yes, the CJCS commands all joint forces during wartime
Yes, but only during contingency operations
Yes, the CJCS commands functional combatant commands only
No, the CJCS serves in an advisory capacity and does not exercise command authority

questions.question: What type of command authority does a combatant commander exercise over assigned forces?
options: Administrative control (ADCON)
Tactical control (TACON)
Combatant command (COCOM) authority
Coordinating authority

questions.question: A joint force commander (JFC) may establish a joint task force (JTF) when an operation requires what?
options: A single-service response with unified logistics support
Congressional approval for overseas deployment
A joint force with a designated commander to accomplish a specific mission
Only naval and air assets operating in the same theater

questions.question: Which principle of joint operations ensures all efforts are directed toward a clearly defined, decisive, and achievable goal?
options: Unity of command
Mass
Offensive
Objective

questions.question: The principle of 'unity of command' in joint operations means what?
options: All forces must be from the same military service
A single commander directs and coordinates the actions of all forces toward a common objective
Each service component retains independent command of its own forces
Command authority is shared equally among all component commanders

questions.question: Which principle of joint operations involves concentrating the effects of combat power at the most advantageous place and time?
options: Economy of force
Maneuver
Surprise
Mass

questions.question: What does the principle of 'economy of force' require in joint operations?
options: Using maximum force at all points of engagement simultaneously
Reducing the overall size of the joint force to minimize costs
Allocating minimum essential combat power to secondary efforts to mass forces elsewhere
Distributing supplies equally among all components regardless of mission needs

questions.question: The principle of 'maneuver' in joint operations focuses on what?
options: Placing the enemy in a disadvantageous position through the flexible application of combat power
Conducting only defensive operations until attacked
Moving forces exclusively by air to gain speed
Stationing forces in fixed defensive positions

questions.question: Which principle of joint operations involves striking the enemy at a time, place, or in a manner for which they are not prepared?
options: Security
Offensive
Surprise
Simplicity

questions.question: The principle of 'security' in joint operations is primarily about what?
options: Never allowing the enemy to acquire an unexpected advantage
Preventing unauthorized personnel from entering military installations
Classifying all operational documents at the highest level
Assigning security forces to every unit in the joint force

questions.question: Why is 'simplicity' considered a principle of joint operations?
options: It limits the number of forces that can participate in an operation
It requires using only one type of weapon system per engagement
It reduces the chances of miscommunication in multinational coalitions
It increases the probability that plans and orders will be clearly understood and properly executed

questions.question: Which principle of joint operations calls for the measured use of force appropriate to the situation?
options: Restraint
Legitimacy
Perseverance
Offensive

questions.question: The principle of 'perseverance' in joint operations emphasizes what?
options: The willingness to withdraw forces at the first sign of enemy resistance
Rotating forces frequently to maintain morale
Accepting any terms offered by an adversary to end conflict quickly
The patient, resolute, and persistent pursuit of national objectives despite obstacles

questions.question: The principle of 'legitimacy' in joint operations is most closely related to what?
options: Ensuring operations conform to domestic law only
Maintaining legal and moral authority in the conduct of operations
Gaining approval from the United Nations before any military action
Using only conventional weapons in all engagements

questions.question: The principle of 'offensive' in joint operations means what?
options: Avoiding all engagement with the enemy until full force readiness is achieved
Conducting exclusively air-to-air combat operations
Using only special operations forces for initial engagement
Seizing, retaining, and exploiting the initiative to gain and maintain a position of advantage

questions.question: What is the joint force air component commander (JFACC) responsible for?
options: Making recommendations to the JFC on the proper employment of air forces and, if delegated, planning and executing joint air operations
Commanding all ground forces in a joint operations area
Managing the defense budget for the joint force
Coordinating only strategic airlift missions

questions.question: Who typically designates the JFACC in a joint force?
options: The Secretary of the Air Force
The Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
The theater air control system commander
The joint force commander (JFC)

questions.question: How many phases are in the joint phasing model for operations?
options: Three (Deter, Seize Initiative, Dominate)
Four (Shape, Deter, Dominate, Stabilize)
Five (Shape, Deter, Seize Initiative, Dominate, Transition)
Six (Shape, Deter, Seize Initiative, Dominate, Stabilize, Enable Civil Authority)

questions.question: In the joint phasing model, which phase focuses on setting conditions to deter adversaries and assure allies?
options: Phase 0 — Shape
Phase I — Deter
Phase II — Seize Initiative
Phase III — Dominate

questions.question: Phase V of the joint phasing model is called what?
options: Dominate
Stabilize
Transition
Enable Civil Authority

questions.question: What is the Adaptive Planning and Execution (APEX) system?
options: The Department of Defense system for joint operational planning and execution
A personnel management system for joint duty assignments
A fitness tracking system used by joint forces
A logistics tool for tracking supplies across combatant commands

questions.question: APEX replaced which earlier joint planning system?
options: Joint Strategic Planning System (JSPS)
Joint Operation Planning and Execution System (JOPES)
Contingency Planning Guidance (CPG)
Global Force Management (GFM)

questions.question: What is Enlisted Joint Professional Military Education (EJPME)?
options: A program that replaces service-specific PME for all enlisted members
Training provided only to officers serving in joint billets
A deployment readiness course required before all overseas tours
Education designed to prepare enlisted members for joint duty assignments and develop joint competencies

questions.question: Which phase of EJPME is integrated into the Senior NCO Academy curriculum?
options: EJPME Phase I
EJPME Phase II
EJPME Phase III
EJPME is not integrated into any PME curriculum

questions.question: What is a joint duty assignment?
options: An assignment to a joint activity on the joint duty assignment list, involving multiple military departments
An assignment to a unit composed of two or more Air Force specialty codes
Any overseas assignment regardless of the organization
A temporary duty assignment to a sister service training school

questions.question: In multinational operations, what is the primary challenge compared to purely joint operations?
options: Multinational operations require fewer forces overall
Differences in language, culture, doctrine, equipment, and national objectives add complexity
All participating nations must use the same weapons systems
Multinational operations eliminate the need for a unified command structure

questions.question: What is the purpose of interagency coordination in joint operations?
options: To replace military forces with civilian agency personnel in combat zones
To synchronize and integrate activities of governmental and nongovernmental organizations with military operations
To eliminate the role of the Department of State in national security
To transfer command authority from the military to a civilian agency

questions.question: What is the chain of command for military operations from the President down to combatant commanders?
options: President to CJCS to combatant commanders
President to National Security Advisor to combatant commanders
President to Secretary of Defense to combatant commanders
President to Service Secretaries to combatant commanders

questions.question: Which geographic combatant command is responsible for military operations in the Middle East?
options: U.S. European Command (USEUCOM)
U.S. Africa Command (USAFRICOM)
U.S. Central Command (USCENTCOM)
U.S. Southern Command (USSOUTHCOM)

questions.question: What distinguishes operational control (OPCON) from combatant command (COCOM) authority?
options: OPCON is broader than COCOM and includes administrative authority
OPCON includes authority to organize and employ commands as the commander considers necessary, but cannot be transferred and is less comprehensive than COCOM
COCOM may be transferred to subordinate commanders while OPCON may not
OPCON is inherent to combatant commanders and cannot be delegated, while COCOM can be delegated

questions.question: What is tactical control (TACON)?
options: The broadest command authority granted to combatant commanders
Authority over all administrative and logistic functions of assigned forces
Command authority over assigned forces that includes the detailed direction of movements and maneuvers necessary to accomplish assigned missions
Authority to reorganize a combatant command's force structure

questions.question: During Phase III (Dominate) of the joint phasing model, what is the primary focus?
options: Setting conditions for a favorable security environment
Deterring adversary actions through show of force
Breaking the enemy's will to resist, establishing control over the operational environment, and completing full-spectrum operations
Transferring authority to civilian governance

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of U.S. Transportation Command (USTRANSCOM)?
options: It serves as a geographic combatant command covering North American territory
It provides global air, land, and sea transportation for the Department of Defense
It commands all nuclear forces for the United States
It conducts cyber operations and defends DOD information networks

questions.question: EJPME Phase I is integrated into which enlisted PME course?
options: NCO Academy
Airman Leadership School
Chief Leadership Course
First Sergeant Academy
